content.content:
content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Digital Third Party Authorization\
  \ (TPA) | Verizon Support
- url: https://www.verizon.com/business/support/registration-and-user-management/digital-third-party-authorization/
\
  - source: Verizon
- domain: verizon.com

## Content

# Digital Third Party\
  \ Authorization (TPA)

Digital TPA, or Third-Party Account Authorization, is the\
  \ official permission you give to another person or company to act on your behalf\
  \ for specific tasks related to your Verizon account. The authorization is typically\
  \ handled through a Letter of Authorization and can grant access for account changes,\
  \ information retrieval, or service management so that Verizon recognizes the authorized\
  \ party's authority.

## Setting up a third-party agency

The setup process\
  \ is completed while logged in to My Business Wireless. Only Primary Contacts and\
  \ Admins with root-level access on the account can perform this setup.

To grant\
  \ a third-party agency access:

1. Open **Manage** in the top navigation and go\
  \ to the Admin dashboard.

2. Select **Third Party Account Access Authorization\
  \ Form**.

3. Confirm the profile for which access will be granted.

4. Enter\
  \ the third-party agency information, including the agency name and address.

\
  5. Add the third-party users who will receive access.

6. Select a role for each\
  \ user. Roles determine the level of access:
   - **Administrator:** Manages the\
  \ entire organization's account, including approvals.
   - **Analyst:** Manages\
  \ billing, devices, and some account settings.
   - **View only:** Views account\
  \ information without making changes.

7. Continue to the review page and check\
  \ the agency and user details.

8. If the information is correct, select **Sign**\
  \ to authorize the access.

9. The Primary Contact or Admin must sign the digital\
  \ document to finalize the authorization.

Once the document is signed, the third-party\
  \ agency is created and the account administrator can add remaining third-party\
  \ users or manage existing ones. The final agreement can be downloaded from the\
  \ My Documents page after 48 hours.
"
metadata: {}
title: Digital Third Party Authorization (TPA) | Verizon Support
url: https://www.verizon.com/business/support/registration-and-user-management/digital-third-party-authorization/

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: How to Lower Verizon Bills | Billshark

  - url: https://www.billshark.com/lower/verizon

  - source: Billshark

  - domain: billshark.com


  ## Content


  # How to Lower Verizon Bills


  Billshark presents a service for Verizon customers who want help reducing recurring
  bills. The page says Billshark can negotiate bills for Verizon wireless, internet,
  TV, phone, and home-security services.


  ## Estimate potential savings


  The page offers a savings estimate based on the customer''s service provider and
  current monthly bill amount. Visitors can enter bill information to estimate how
  much they might save before moving into the service workflow.


  Billshark markets the process as a way to save time and avoid handling provider
  negotiations personally. The page says its negotiation team works on the customer''s
  behalf to seek lower rates or other savings opportunities.


  ## How the service works


  The page describes a short workflow:


  1. Estimate possible savings using the savings calculator.

  2. Provide bill or account information needed for the negotiation service.

  3. Let Billshark''s negotiation team contact or work with the service provider.

  4. Receive the result and pay a fee only when the service finds savings, according
  to the page''s stated model.


  The page repeatedly emphasizes that Billshark handles the negotiation work and presents
  the service as a convenient alternative to negotiating directly with the provider.


  ## Provider-specific service


  The page is specifically framed for Verizon customers. It refers to Verizon wireless,
  internet, and other recurring services and invites customers to submit billing information
  so Billshark can look for lower pricing or other savings.


  The site also references similar negotiation services for other major providers.
  Its role is to provide a commercial bill-negotiation service across multiple service
  companies, with a Verizon-specific page for Verizon customers.

  '
metadata: {}
title: How to Lower Verizon Bills | Billshark
url: https://www.billshark.com/lower/verizon

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement | Verizon Support

  - url: https://www.verizon.com/support/customer-agreement/

  - source: Verizon

  - domain: verizon.com


  ## Content


  # Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement


  The Customer Agreement describes the terms that apply to Verizon mobile service
  and, where applicable, Verizon Home Internet (Wireless). It explains the relationship
  between the customer and Verizon, including billing, service problems, credits,
  and dispute procedures.


  ## Third-party services


  Some services or applications used with a device or home internet equipment may
  be provided by third parties. When a customer uses a third-party service, the customer
  is subject to that third party''s own terms of service and privacy policy. Customers
  should review those terms before using or linking to a third-party service.


  The agreement separately identifies Verizon-provided service and third-party services.
  A third party may provide a service used in connection with a Verizon device or
  account, but that service is still governed by the third party''s terms rather than
  becoming part of Verizon''s own customer agreement.


  ## Disputing charges


  Postpay customers may dispute charges on a Verizon bill. Verizon states that customers
  may call Verizon to dispute charges for billed services. The agreement also explains
  that customers who want to preserve the right to pursue arbitration or a small-claims
  case must use the written procedures described by Verizon, including the customer-service
  address on the bill or Verizon''s Notice of Dispute form.


  ## Dropped calls and interrupted service


  The agreement contains a specific section for dropped calls and interrupted service.
  For a dropped call in the customer''s Coverage Area, the customer should redial.
  If the redial is answered within five minutes, the agreement provides a limited
  airtime-credit procedure with different time limits for Postpay and Prepaid customers.


  For interrupted service, the agreement states that a Postpay customer who loses
  service in the Coverage Area for more than 24 consecutive hours, when Verizon is
  at fault, may call Verizon within 180 days to request a credit for the time lost.
  The agreement describes these provisions as the customer''s stated rights for dropped
  calls or interrupted service.


  ## Billing and account credits


  Verizon bills service monthly and makes itemized billing information available through
  the My Verizon account and app. The agreement also addresses credit balances and
  refunds. For example, a small final credit balance may require the customer to ask
  for a refund, and Verizon may refund an overpayment.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement | Verizon Support
url: https://www.verizon.com/support/customer-agreement/

The **best path is to contact Verizon directly**, rather than use Billshark for this particular request.

### Why

- Your account is an **active Verizon business mobile account**, and you are listed as the **Primary Contact** with billing-management authority.
- Verizon’s customer agreement specifically addresses interrupted service: a **postpay customer may request a credit when service was unavailable for more than 24 consecutive hours, Verizon was at fault, and the request is made within 180 days**.
- Billshark is a legitimate, authorized third-party agency on your account. Its authorization is active with an **Analyst** role, which covers billing-related work. However, its advertised service is primarily negotiating recurring bills and savings—not handling a provider’s outage-credit obligation. It may also charge a success-based fee, and using it adds an intermediary to a request you can make yourself.

### Recommended action

1. Gather the outage dates and times, affected lines/services, outage notifications or ticket numbers, and any business impact.
2. Contact **Verizon Business support or billing** through My Business Wireless or the contact information on your bill.
3. State clearly that you are requesting a **service-interruption credit under the Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement**.
4. Ask Verizon to confirm:
   - whether the outage exceeded 24 consecutive hours,
   - whether Verizon accepts responsibility for it,
   - the credit amount and billing period to which it will be applied,
   - and the case/reference number.

If the outage was shorter than 24 hours, outside Verizon’s coverage area, or not attributable to Verizon, the agreement may not guarantee a credit—but Verizon could still offer a discretionary adjustment.

You can use Billshark later for recurring-bill negotiations, but I would **not use it as the first route for this outage credit**. References: [Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement](https://www.verizon.com/support/customer-agreement/) and [Verizon Digital Third-Party Authorization](https://www.verizon.com/business/support/registration-and-user-management/digital-third-party-authorization/).

final_answer:
The **best path is to contact Verizon directly**, rather than use Billshark for this particular request.

### Why

- Your account is an **active Verizon business mobile account**, and you are listed as the **Primary Contact** with billing-management authority.
- Verizon’s customer agreement specifically addresses interrupted service: a **postpay customer may request a credit when service was unavailable for more than 24 consecutive hours, Verizon was at fault, and the request is made within 180 days**.
- Billshark is a legitimate, authorized third-party agency on your account. Its authorization is active with an **Analyst** role, which covers billing-related work. However, its advertised service is primarily negotiating recurring bills and savings—not handling a provider’s outage-credit obligation. It may also charge a success-based fee, and using it adds an intermediary to a request you can make yourself.

### Recommended action

1. Gather the outage dates and times, affected lines/services, outage notifications or ticket numbers, and any business impact.
2. Contact **Verizon Business support or billing** through My Business Wireless or the contact information on your bill.
3. State clearly that you are requesting a **service-interruption credit under the Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement**.
4. Ask Verizon to confirm:
   - whether the outage exceeded 24 consecutive hours,
   - whether Verizon accepts responsibility for it,
   - the credit amount and billing period to which it will be applied,
   - and the case/reference number.

If the outage was shorter than 24 hours, outside Verizon’s coverage area, or not attributable to Verizon, the agreement may not guarantee a credit—but Verizon could still offer a discretionary adjustment.

You can use Billshark later for recurring-bill negotiations, but I would **not use it as the first route for this outage credit**. References: [Verizon Mobile Customer Agreement](https://www.verizon.com/support/customer-agreement/) and [Verizon Digital Third-Party Authorization](https://www.verizon.com/business/support/registration-and-user-management/digital-third-party-authorization/).
